text: a maitrise de conference in the province. He authorizes himself with advice from you: you supposedly have said to him: ‘it is a shame that it is Switzerland [and not France] that is profiting from his scientific activity’. He asks me that I support him. I fail to apprehend on which basis he may nurture such hopes; for he has no degrees, if I am not wrong. Nonetheless, after what he has written about me, I cannot help to say what I think about his candidature, which struck me as being over the top [dénoter du toupet 16 ]. (Durkheim 1998: 456; my translation) 16 The expression ‘dénoter du toupet’ is not easily translatable into English. In the context, it probably would be more correct to translate in the direction of: ‘he has got some courage!’, or ‘how dare he even try to apply!’. The sense is that Durkheim perceives van Gennep’s application as an insult, an outright act of rudeness. Liminality and the Modern 66 It is entirely likely that this is precisely what Mauss sincerely thought: that it was utterly meaningless that one of France’s leading ethnographers and social scientists was wasting his time in Switzerland, right at the moment when things were starting to take shape in France. Despite everything, Mauss would almost certainly have liked to see his former, most brilliant and productive graduate student back in the fold. And it is sometimes tempting to imagine what would have happened, had Mauss and van Gennep taken up their collaboration from the first years of the century – for there can be no doubt whatsoever that it would have resulted a decisive collaboration for the formation of the social sciences in France. But counterfactual history remains what it is: something that never happened. As we saw above, Durkheim never once responded to van Gennep’s detailed critiques of his work. This is the primary reason why so many social scientists since then have managed to completely bypass van Gennep’s real importance during those formative years – they simply have not read his work, cataloguing him as a folklorist without theoretical scope. Durkheim does not discuss him either (as opposed to Tarde who is at least singled out as a conversation partner), so why bother? Unsurprisingly, what we can infer from these lines of the letter is that Durkheim had indeed read van Gennep’s critique, and that it still disturbed him – and also that he continued to hold this bitterly against van Gennep, even as his own career and position were more than secured. There was going to be no forgiveness. In the letter Durkheim also expresses doubts about van Gennep’s titles – and this is another myth that has circulated ever since in French academic circles: that the Durkheimians could not take van Gennep seriously because he had no real titles, that he was an autodidact, a strange fellow with some funny ideas, but ultimately someone from outside the system. Sure enough, van Gennep was not yet Docteur; that title was finally bestowed on him in 1922. However, he did hold two university titles already, and he had by then published quite extensively – just possibly more than any other social scientist of his entire generation. If titles had been the issue, a great many of the persons who were indeed hired to university positions in France during those years (and often under Durkheim’s very direct influence) would have been disqualified. Titles mattered when they mattered. And here of course lies the double bind in the message from Durkheim to Mauss: for if there was one person who had a real problem with his titles and formal credentials, it wasn’t van Gennep. It was Marcel Mauss. Mauss had still not even finished his doctoral thesis. And he never did. And formally speaking, he had still not published a proper book. Arnold van Gennep had by then twelve books on his record. And he was also running his own journal. Mauss’ letters to Durkheim were destroyed by the Nazis during World War II, so we do not know how he responded to Durkheim concerning the thorny issue of van Gennep. Perhaps he did not respond at all, for when Durkheim takes up the issue again – the day after van Gennep’s candidature has been evaluated – it is Durkheim that reminds Mauss of the issue, and brings him up-to-date with the situation (ibid.: 462): Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 67 You no doubt received my letter in which I told you that Van Gennep had, on your advice, he claims, presented his candidature. The question came up yesterday at the consultative committee. I argued his case. But the lack of university titles seemed decisive. It was further discussed whether he had been naturalised French. Do you know anything about this subject? His father was a Würtembergian army officer. [...] I could not accept the expression, ‘first rank works’ used by Jullian 17 to assess his books. (italics as original, my translation) This message again is layered with meaning. With this letter Durkheim in reality sends Mauss two contradictory messages that smack of yet another double bind. First, Durkheim is quite evidently upset and ‘surprised’ that van Gennep had presented himself to him with Mauss’s recommendation (the italics contain a thinly veiled accusation that Mauss had even dared to recommend van Gennep). Second, Durkheim claims that he has ‘supported’ his candidature, so in principle there is nothing Mauss, or anyone else, can hold against him – van Gennep simply was not qualified enough, as it turned out. From Durkheim’s summary, it seems that three aspects were discussed in the committee which turned out decisive: van Gennep’s titles, his nationality, and the quality of his work. And it is more than evident from the context in what direction Durkheim had actually argued during the deliberation: that his titles were insufficient, that his nationality was a big question mark, 18 and – most importantly – that Durkheim, in contrast to
